# Executive Summary {-}

## An inflection point we have seen before

In the early 1920s, the commercial possibilities of radio broadcasting became apparent almost simultaneously in the United States, the United Kingdom, and across Europe. Left to market forces, radio quickly consolidated around advertising-funded networks that prioritised entertainment over public information. The response, in Britain and then across much of Europe, was deliberate: the creation of public broadcasting institutions — the BBC chartered in 1927, followed by analogous bodies across Scandinavia, the Netherlands, and Germany — whose explicit mandate was to serve the public interest rather than commercial shareholders. The decision was not automatic or inevitable. It required political will, coalition-building among cultural institutions and civic reformers, and the articulation of a compelling alternative vision. That window of institutional possibility was narrow. Where it was seized, public broadcasting became foundational infrastructure. Where it was not, commercial interests locked in patterns that persist to this day [@starr2004_creation].

We are at an equivalent inflection point for social media. The concentrated, advertising-dependent platforms that have dominated the past two decades are visibly failing the public interest: eroding epistemic commons, monetising attention and personal data, and proving structurally resistant to democratic accountability [@zuboff2019_surveillance]. At the same moment, a mature, interoperable, decentralised alternative exists and is growing — the Fediverse. The question, as it was for radio a century ago, is whether civil society, public institutions, and forward-looking funders will act in time to consolidate and support it.

## What is the Fediverse, and why now?

The open, federated social web — known as the Fediverse — hosts millions of users across thousands of independently operated servers running on the ActivityPub protocol [@w3c_activitypub_2018]. Yet this infrastructure remains fragile: underfunded, under-governed, and largely invisible to the philanthropic and public-sector funders who are actively searching for exactly what it represents: trustworthy, democratic, non-commercial public digital infrastructure.

This brief makes the case for forming a **Fediverse Coalition** — a structured alliance of infrastructure operators, public-interest technology organisations, civic institutions, and academic partners — to collectively secure the Fediverse's long-term sustainability and position it as a model for public digital goods at the European and global level.

## A long genealogy: this is not a new idea, but a maturing one

The Fediverse did not emerge from nowhere. It is the latest and most successful expression of a decades-long effort to build internet communication infrastructure that is open, interoperable, and resistant to centralisation. Understanding this lineage matters for funders: it demonstrates that the underlying vision is robust and time-tested, not utopian or experimental.

The story begins in the early 1980s with **Usenet** — a globally distributed discussion network running over dial-up connections, whose architecture of news servers propagating messages peer-to-peer anticipated modern federation in almost every structural respect [@hauben1997_netizens]. Through the 1990s, **IRC (Internet Relay Chat)** scaled federated real-time messaging to millions of concurrent users across independently operated servers, demonstrating that federation could handle significant load without central coordination. In the early 2000s, **XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol)**, originally known as Jabber, established a fully open, standards-based federation protocol for instant messaging; at its peak it formed the backbone of Google Talk, Facebook Chat, and was adopted by the IETF as a formal internet standard [@saint-andre2011_xmpp]. Each of these systems demonstrated the viability of federated architecture; each also suffered from the absence of coordinated governance and sustainable funding that a coalition could provide.

The direct ancestry of the contemporary Fediverse runs through **StatusNet** (2008) and its successor **GNU Social**, which implemented the **OStatus** protocol stack — combining Atom feeds, Salmon, WebFinger, and PubSubHubbub to enable cross-server social networking. The critical institutional moment came with the formation of the **W3C Social Web Working Group** (2014–2018), which brought together protocol designers, platform operators, and standards experts to consolidate these approaches into a single, W3C-blessed specification. The result was **ActivityPub**, published as a W3C Recommendation in January 2018 [@w3c_activitypub_2018]. The Mastodon platform's adoption of ActivityPub provided the network effect that earlier federated social systems had lacked [@zignani2018_fediverse]. By 2023, high-profile departures from Twitter/X had driven Mastodon and the broader Fediverse past ten million registered accounts, with institutional adopters including the European Commission, the German federal government, and dozens of universities deploying their own instances [@fediverse_observer_2024].

The trajectory is clear: what began as a technical experiment has become a credible alternative. The missing piece is not technical — it is organisational and financial.

## Theoretical grounding: commons governance is not utopian

Sceptics sometimes characterise community-governed digital infrastructure as idealistic — an amateur enterprise that cannot match the reliability or scale of commercial platforms. The political economy literature offers a robust rebuttal.

Elinor Ostrom, awarded the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences in 2009, spent her career documenting how communities successfully govern shared resources — fisheries, irrigation systems, forests — outside both the market and the state. Her eight design principles for sustainable commons governance [@ostrom1990_governing] — including clearly defined boundaries, congruent rules, collective-choice arrangements, monitoring, graduated sanctions, and recognised rights to organise — are not abstract ideals. They are empirical generalisations drawn from hundreds of case studies of commons that have survived for decades or centuries. The Fediverse's structure of community-operated instances, evolving community standards, and protocol governance through open standards bodies maps naturally onto Ostrom's framework. The coalition proposed in this brief is precisely the institutional layer that Ostrom's research identifies as necessary for a commons to move from informal resilience to durable long-term sustainability.

Yochai Benkler's concept of **commons-based peer production** [@benkler2006_wealth] provides a complementary frame. Benkler demonstrated, through analysis of projects including Linux, Wikipedia, and the Apache web server, that large-scale, high-quality information goods can be produced by distributed networks of voluntary contributors coordinating through open protocols — without the price signals of markets or the commands of hierarchies. The Fediverse is commons-based peer production applied to social infrastructure. Benkler's work makes clear that this is not a fringe phenomenon: it is a structurally distinct and economically significant mode of production that has already generated some of the most widely used software and content in human history. What it requires, as Benkler and Ostrom both emphasise, is appropriate institutional support — the kind of support this coalition is designed to provide.

## What success looks like: a 2030 vision

If this coalition is funded and executes on its mission, what does the Fediverse look like in five years?

**Users and adoption.** By 2030, the Fediverse hosts 50–100 million active users, up from an estimated 10–12 million in 2024. Growth is driven not only by disillusioned users of commercial platforms but by the institutional adoption of ActivityPub-compatible software by universities, public broadcasters, municipal governments, and cultural institutions. The coalition's certification programme has established a recognisable quality mark for Fediverse operators, making institutional adoption a low-risk decision.

**Institutional deployments.** At least 20 European universities operate public-facing Mastodon or ActivityPub instances as part of their research communication infrastructure. Public broadcasters in at least three EU member states run ActivityPub-enabled news and media accounts, syndicating verified content into the federated social web without algorithmic intermediaries. The European Commission's existing Mastodon presence has been joined by the Parliament and the Council, establishing the Fediverse as the default European intergovernmental social communication channel.

**Policy outcomes.** The Digital Services Act's provisions on interoperability [@european_commission_dsa_2022] have been successfully interpreted — with the coalition's technical and legal input — to require large commercial social platforms to implement ActivityPub gateways, allowing their users to follow and interact with Fediverse accounts without switching platforms. This is the structural intervention that turns the Fediverse from an alternative into an interconnected part of the broader social web. The coalition has submitted formal responses to at least three European Commission consultations on platform regulation, establishing it as a recognised stakeholder in digital policy.

**Protocol evolution.** The coalition funds a standing technical working group that has published two significant extensions to the ActivityPub protocol, addressing known limitations in content discovery and group management. These extensions are implemented across the five largest Fediverse software projects and have been submitted to the W3C Social Web Community Group as candidate additions to the standard. The coalition maintains a public test suite that allows instance operators to verify protocol compliance.

**Financial sustainability.** The coalition's secretariat is financially self-sustaining through a mix of membership fees from institutional members, project grants, and a small infrastructure levy on commercially operated ActivityPub services. The dependence on philanthropic grant funding has fallen from 100% in year one to under 40% by year five, demonstrating a path to permanent institutional independence.

This is not a speculative scenario. Each of these outcomes has a precedent in the trajectory of other public interest digital infrastructure — from the transition of the Linux kernel from hobbyist project to enterprise foundation to the establishment of the Internet Archive as a permanent institution [@kelty2008_two]. The question is not whether these outcomes are achievable. It is whether the necessary coalition-building and funding work begins now, while the window is still open.

## What this brief contains

- **Chapter 1 — Brand & Identity:** Candidate coalition names, brand pillars, and a messaging matrix tailored to different funder audiences.
- **Chapter 2 — Partners:** A shortlist of anchor organisations with roles, rationale, and engagement status.
- **Chapter 3 — Pitch Deck Outline:** A structured narrative — problem, solution, coalition model, funding ask, impact — ready to be turned into slides.
- **Chapter 4 — Funding Landscape:** A scan of relevant European and international programmes, their buzzwords, and fit scores.
- **Chapter 5 — Outreach Plan:** Templates, timeline, and approach for approaching funders in sequence.
- **Appendix A — Artefacts Checklist:** Everything needed for a full funding proposal.
- **Appendix B — Theoretical & Historical References:** An annotated bibliography of the foundational literature on federated public infrastructure.

## The ask in one sentence

We seek **€1.2–2.5 million over three years** to establish a coalition secretariat, fund infrastructure maintenance, and commission the research and governance work that makes the Fediverse ready for public and institutional adoption at scale.
